turns.content: I'm trying to parse a binary protocol using the deku crate, but I can't seem to figure out how to define my struct so it actually works with the derive macros.

You're looking at the right tool. With deku you just need an attribute on your struct and a field that implements Into<u8> or u8, then you can use the Derive macro to generate the parsing logic automatically.
So I should add #[derive(PartialEq)] deku::Deku before my struct and then reference the fields in the body of the derive macro?

Not quite. The Deke trait doesn't have a generic derive attribute like that. You use #[derive(deku::Deke)] on your struct, not PartialEq. Then you declare fields inside the derive block with attributes like deku::BitField or deku::U8 to tell deku how to handle each field.
Okay, so I'm defining a struct and then using the Deke trait to parse it. But what if my binary protocol uses variable-length fields or nested structures? Can deku handle that?

Yes, but you need to be careful with nesting. You can define a separate struct for each layer and reference it from the parent using deku::BitField. For variable-length fields you use deku::U8 or deku::U16 depending on how many bytes the field takes.
What if I want to parse multiple structs in a single message? Like an array of packets, can deku handle that?
You can use deku::U32 or deku::U16 to count the number of items and then iterate over them with deku::BitField. You'll need a loop in your parsing logic that calls deku for each packet.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to add a new protocol header but it feels like I'm manually writing out every single bit field when I want something declarative. Can someone explain how deku actually handles the parsing?

Great question, that frustration is totally normal for your first time with this crate. The core magic happens because deku derives a specific trait called DekuCodec from your struct definition using derive macros. When you implement the DekuCodec trait on your data type, deku automatically generates the parsing and serialization logic for all those bit fields without needing any boilerplate code. It uses the DekuParser internally to read raw binary streams, so once your Rust structs are marked with the right attributes, deku takes over the heavy lifting of interpreting each byte as a sequence of bits according to your protocol specification.
Hey, I'm back after a break and trying to onboard on this new codebase using the deku crate. I got an error saying derive macro failed for my struct and asked what went wrong.

That usually means your struct is missing some required attributes or has invalid field names that deku can't parse. Did you try using the standard derive macro syntax on a plain struct without any custom logic?
Yeah, I just added the derive macro to my struct but it still failed. I assumed since I was defining fields like u8 and i16 that deku would automatically handle everything.

Ah, you're assuming deku will auto-detect field types without explicit configuration, which isn't how it works. You need to manually specify the bit-width for each field in the derive macro call so deku knows exactly what to expect from your binary data.
